preamble: The shift from a glottal stop [ʔ] to a glottal fricative [h] is a process of glottal spirantization. This is a form of lenition where the complete closure of the vocal folds (the stop) weakens into a partial closure that allows air to escape, creating audible friction (the fricative). This shift is particularly common in Afroasiatic and Polynesian languages.

examples.note: Occurs in certain dialects, particularly in unstressed or final positions where the closure is incomplete.
Documented weakening of glottal stops in various island dialects.

sources: Watson, J. C. (2002). The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Holes, C. (2004). Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties.
